title: akrun Analytics
text:
Welcome to akrun Analytics, where data transforms into actionable insights, and innovation fuels our passion for business analytics. Meet Arun Kirshna Sasikala Appukuttan, our distinguished Chief Data Scientist, leading the charge in converting raw data into strategic intelligence.
Arun’s dedication to the realm of data is unparalleled. When not spearheading advancements in data platforms, he immerses himself in Stack Overflow, ranking in the top 0.01% overall. His commitment to knowledge sharing is evident with over 37,000 statistics-related answers, making him an invaluable global resource.
Beyond providing insights, Arun is the all-time top R contributor on Stack Overflow, showcasing mastery in statistical programming and influencing the data science landscape.
At akrun Analytics, we leverage Arun’s expertise to navigate business analytics complexities. With keen innovation and insatiable curiosity, Arun empowers businesses through data-driven decision-making. Join us on this transformative journey, where data meets excellence, and let akrun Analytics be your guiding compass. Together, we elevate your data strategies, driving unprecedented success in the dynamic world of business analytics.


Donna Tartt -
 What are the dead, anyway, but waves and 
energy? Light shining from a dead star?That, by 
the way, is a phrase of Julian's. 
I remember it from a lecture of his 
on the Iliad, when Patroklos appears to Achilles 
in a dream. 
There is a very moving passage where Achilles 
overjoyed at the sight of the apparition – 
tries to throw his arms around the ghost 
of his old friend, and it vanishes. 
The dead appear to us in dreams, said 
Julian, because that's the only way they can 
make us see them; what we see is 
only a projection, beamed from a great distance, 
light shining at us from a dead star…Which 
reminds me, by the way, of a dream 
I had a couple of weeks ago.I 
found myself in a strange deserted city – 
an old city, like London – underpopulated by 
war or disease. 
It was night; the streets were dark, bombed-out, 
abandoned. 
For a long time, I wandered aimlessly – 
past ruined parks, blasted statuary, vacant lots overgrown 
with weeds and collapsed apartment houses with rusted 
girders poking out of their sides like ribs. 
But here and there, interspersed among the desolate 
shells of the heavy old public buildings, I 
began to see new buildings, too, which were 
connected by futuristic walkways lit from beneath. 
Long, cool perspectives of modern architecture, rising phosphorescent 
and eerie from the rubble.I 
went inside one of these new buildings. 
It was like a laboratory, maybe, or a 
museum. 
My footsteps echoed on the tile floors.There 
was a cluster of men, all smoking pipes, 
gathered around an exhibit in a glass case 
that gleamed in the dim light and lit 
their faces ghoulishly from below.I 
drew nearer. 
In the case was a machine revolving slowly 
on a turntable, a machine with metal parts 
that slid in and out and collapsed in 
upon themselves to form new images. 
An Inca temple… click click click… the Pyramids… 
the Parthenon.History 
passing beneath my very eyes, changing every moment.'I 
thought I'd find you here,' said a voice 
at my elbow.It 
was Henry. 
His gaze was steady and impassive in the 
dim light. 
Above his ear, beneath the wire stem of 
his spectacles, I could just make out the 
powder burn and the dark hole in his 
right temple.I 
was glad to see him, though not exactly 
surprised. 
'You know,' I said to him, 'everybody is 
saying that you're dead.'He 
stared down at the machine. 
The Colosseum… click click click… the Pantheon. 
'I'm not dead,' he said. 
'I'm only having a bit of trouble with 
my passport.''What?'He 
cleared his throat. 
'My movements are restricted,' he said.'I 
no longer have the ability to travel as 
freely as I would like.'Hagia 
Sophia. 
St. 
Mark's, in Venice. 
'What is this place?' I asked him.'That 
information is classified, I'm afraid.'1 
looked around curiously. 
It seemed that I was the only visitor.'Is 
it open to the public?' I said.'Not 
generally, no.'I 
looked at him. 
There was so much I wanted to ask 
him, so much I wanted to say; but 
somehow I knew there wasn't time and even 
if there was, that it was all, somehow, 
beside the point.'Are 
you happy here?' I said at last.He 
considered this for a moment. 
'Not particularly,' he said.'But 
you're not very happy where you are, either.'St. 
Basil's, in Moscow. 
Chartres. 
Salisbury and Amiens. 
He glanced at his watch.'I 
hope you'll excuse me,' he said, 'but I'm 
late for an appointment.'He 
turned from me and walked away. 
I watched his back receding down the long, 
gleaming hall.

title: akrun Analytics
text:
Arun Kirshna Sasikala Appukuttan: Analytics pro with 20+ years (10+ industry, 10+ research). Expert in R (15+ yrs), Python (5), SQL (5), Java (2+). Holds a Ph.D. in Quantitative Genetics. Passionate about tech, data, and cutting-edge analytics. Proven track record, dynamic and innovative approach.





University of Maryland | College Park, MD | PhD in Quantitative Genetics | Aug 2006 - May 2010
South Dakota State University | Brooking, SD | MS in Dairy Nutrition | Aug 2004 - Jul 2006



Ames Energy | Head Data Scientist | Oct 2018 - present
Scifluent | Data Scientist|Data Engineer | Jul 2018 - Apr 2020
Accenture | Analytics Advisory Specialist | Sep 2015 - Apr 2018
